Primary required changes

A) Student Voice AI / Russell Group claim

Required action:

- Do not make the claim stronger than the evidence supports.

Append note bullets such as:

B) HEPI student adoption slide
Locate slide id `pwut9ace` ("Student adoption is already mainstream").

Keep the three stats, because they are good:

- "Reference: HEPI reports 95% use AI in at least one way, 94% use generative AI to help with assessed work, and 65% say assessment has changed significantly because of AI."

C) Upstream build-out slide
Locate slide id `yx2lcivr` ("The upstream build-out is vast — and highly concentrated").

     value: "~$1tn"
     label: "announced multi-year AI infrastructure deals and project ceilings shaping the frontier stack"
   - The point is that this is an approximate aggregation, not a single official total.

Then append note references, for example:

```bash
import json
from pathlib import Path
obj=json.loads(Path('deck-generator/content.json').read_text())
for i,s in enumerate(obj['slides'],1):
    title=s.get('title') or s.get('heading') or s.get('section') or s.get('type')
    print(f"{i:02d} {s.get('id')} | {title}")
    for key in ['subtitle','body']:
        v=s.get(key)
        if isinstance(v,str):
            print('   ', key+':', v[:180].replace('\n',' '))
    if 'stats' in s:
        for st in s['stats']:
            if isinstance(st,dict):
                print('    stat:', st.get('value'), '|', st.get('label'))
    if 'points' in s and isinstance(s['points'], list):
        for pt in s['points'][:5]:
            if isinstance(pt,str):
                print('    point:', pt[:160])
    print()
```

```
ID 8svyxotb
heading: Tech companies are building capacity universities may later inherit
body: The current AI boom is not just hype. It is also an enormous build-out of compute, models, tooling and organisational knowledge that may outlast the frenzy. If universities are strategic, some of that capacity can become cheaper access, better tools and more credible public-interest alternatives.
notes:
- The key line is that speculative capital can still build real capacity.
- Point to compute, models, tooling and organisational learning as durable inheritances of the current boom.
- Say that the question is not whether infrastructure is being built; it is who will control what is left behind.
- Compute and tooling: Data centres, GPU fleets, orchestration frameworks and evaluation tooling are being hardened quickly.
- Open models and hardware: Maturing open models and post-boom hardware markets can make sector alternatives more plausible over time.
- Institutional learning: Universities are also learning fast about policy, pedagogy and workflow design while the ecosystem is moving.


ID xxq4u63y
heading: A small number of firms increasingly control every layer
body: AI is not arriving in higher education as a neutral marketplace of interchangeable tools. A concentrated set of firms controls key model, cloud and interface layers, leaving universities mostly downstream users of systems shaped elsewhere.
notes:
- Explain that universities are not buying from a flat market of interchangeable tools.
- Show the layered dependence: model providers, cloud providers, and interface/platform providers.
- Use the scale asymmetry to explain why universities are usually downstream even when they are important customers.
- Model layer: A handful of providers dominate frontier model access and shape the pace of release.
- Infrastructure and interface: Cloud concentration and campus platforms together shape cost, continuity, distribution and defaults.
- Scale asymmetry: Universities will rarely win by outspending the frontier, so leverage has to come from strategy, collaboration and governance.


ID dw3hcdtx
heading: The AI boom creates a window for shared sector capacity
body: As hardware markets soften and open-weight models mature, the opportunity is to build common services around university-specific tasks rather than repeat the frontier race at smaller scale.
notes:
- Make this the practical follow-on from ownership: shared capacity is how the sector avoids every institution solving the same problems alone.
- Stress the modesty of the claim: not frontier-model rivalry, but reliable shared services for bounded university tasks.
- Shared compute pools: As hardware depreciates and supply loosens, some compute may become affordable for shared sector hosting or bounded institutional workloads.
- University-specific services: Open-weight and smaller models are increasingly good enough for retrieval, classification, transcription, analysis and support tasks common in universities.
- Common assurance: Education and social science are needed alongside computer science to define tasks, evaluate harms and shape institutional fit.


ID dxm011jg
title: Distributed Agents for Autonomous Spacecraft
subtitle: 
notes:
- Use this to show I have a machine learning / AI research background, not just a policy interest.
- The point is that I understand AI systems as systems with architecture, coordination, and failure modes.
```

But that's actually a very remarkably different type of bubble than most, right? That is a purely financial bubble. That could be equated maybe with cryptocurrency, right? They're all on a par. The vast majority of bubbles we've had before have actually pushed forward significantly the technology and the ability to do things. So telecommunications, okay, bubbles building out loads of hardware for telephones, for internet, right? Railroads, okay? Tremendous amounts of investment needed and lots of companies have bust. We, if we are smart, can use this as an opportunity to decide which aspects we want to use and where, right? Using consumer, and this is a key point I want to make, using consumer grade tools in an educational research setting is stupid because they're not designed for that, right? The vast majority of bubbles we've had before have actually pushed forward significantly the technology and the ability to do things.
